narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 56% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. You have an impressive profile in several areas: you ranked in the top decile for your Year 1 grades at school, far above the average, your LSAT score of 37 beats the typical 36.7, your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket which is stronger than most, you're a white male planning full-time studies—demographics that align closely with the majority of students in our dataset—and your undergraduate GPA sits at 2.8, though that's a bit below the average 3.21. Despite these strengths, the model focused most heavily on your Year 3 decile ranking of 5, which is right around the average but had the single biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. Coming in second was your standout Year 1 decile of 10, which surprisingly also nudged the odds toward a fail prediction in the model's eyes. Your family's upper-middle income ranked third in influence, likewise contributing toward the failure forecast. Your solid LSAT score was fourth, your white racial background fifth, and your undergraduate GPA sixth—all of these played supporting roles in tipping the scales against you. On a brighter note, being male was the key factor that pushed back toward predicting success, though its influence was smaller than the top concerns. Compared to typical students, your early academic excellence stands out positively, but your dip to average in Year 3 mirrored patterns the model associates more with bar struggles than your peers who maintained or improved. To boost your future chances, prioritize strengthening your later-year performance like in Year 3—perhaps through targeted study habits or tutoring, since that's the most influential factor you can still shape. You might also consider retaking the LSAT to push it even higher, as it carries notable weight. Building on your top Year 1 decile by seeking mentorship or extra challenges could help counteract its unexpected drag. With focused effort on these academic trajectories, you could shift the model's view in your favor next time. Hang in there—your strong start shows real potential.
